title: Vanilla Ice Cream
subtitle: Custard-based vanilla ice cream churned in an ice cream maker
excerpt: A proper custard base: egg yolks, whole milk, double cream, caster sugar, and a vanilla pod cooked to a thick anglaise, cooled, and churned in an ice cream maker until smooth. The result is richer and more flavourful than any bought equivalent and keeps well in the freezer for up to a month.
sourceNotes: Custard-based vanilla ice cream follows the French tradition of glace à la vanille, where a cooked anglaise forms the base for churned ice cream. The technique of using egg yolks to create a rich, stable frozen custard has been standard in Western ice cream making since the eighteenth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Store in a lidded freezer-proof container for up to one month. Transfer to the fridge for 15 minutes before serving to soften slightly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The custard base can be made up to two days ahead and refrigerated. Churn when needed.

content.text: Custard-based ice cream is more work than the no-churn version, but the result is noticeably richer and smoother. The egg yolks provide both richness and a natural emulsification that prevents large ice crystals forming during churning. The custard must be cooked correctly, enough to thicken and pasteurise the eggs, but not so much that it scrambles.

The custard base must be completely cold before churning. Warm custard going into an ice cream maker will warm the bowl and give a loose, icy result. Make the custard the day before if possible, overnight chilling gives the best texture.

Combine {{whole-milk}} of whole milk and {{double-cream}} of double cream in a medium saucepan with the vanilla pod and seeds. Heat over medium heat until just below simmering. Remove from the heat and leave to infuse for 10 minutes.

Whisk 6 egg yolks with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar in a large bowl until pale and thick. Remove the vanilla pod from the warm cream mixture. Pour the warm cream over the eggs in a slow stream, whisking constantly. Return to the saucepan and cook over low heat, stirring continuously with a wooden spoon, for 8, 12 minutes until the custard thickens to coat the back of the spoon and holds a line when you run your finger across it. Do not let it boil. Strain through a fine sieve into a clean bowl, cool completely, then refrigerate for at least 4 hours or overnight.

Churn the cold custard in an ice cream maker according to the manufacturer's instructions until it reaches a soft, scoopable consistency. Transfer to a lidded freezer-proof container and freeze for at least 2 hours before serving.

items.cause: Heat too high or custard left unstirred
items.fix: Keep the heat low throughout. If you see the custard beginning to thicken quickly or steam rising from the bottom, remove from the heat and stir vigorously. Strain through a sieve, small lumps are often caught this way.

items.symptom: Ice cream is icy and crystalline rather than smooth
items.cause: Custard was not cold enough before churning, or ice cream maker bowl was not frozen enough
items.fix: Chill the custard overnight before churning. Follow the ice cream maker instructions for pre-freezing the bowl, usually 12, 24 hours in the freezer.

items.symptom: Ice cream is very soft and won't hold a scoop
items.cause: Insufficient churning time, or too much sugar in the custard
items.fix: Churn until the machine is clearly working hard to turn the ice cream, a soft-set at the paddle stage. Freeze for at least 2 hours after churning to harden.

items.cause: Infusion time too short, or old vanilla pod
items.fix: Infuse for at least 10 minutes. The seeds scraped from the pod should be clearly visible in the custard. A fresh pod will have visible seeds and a strong vanilla scent when split.

content.text: Custard-based vanilla ice cream follows the French tradition of glace à la vanille, where a cooked anglaise base is churned to produce a rich, smooth frozen cream. The French technique became the international standard for premium ice cream in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, adopted by Italian gelaterie, British dairy ice cream producers, and American artisan makers alike. The vanilla pod itself became the quality marker that distinguishes custard-based ice cream from the artificially flavoured alternatives. The vanilla used in this recipe should have visible seeds, what cooks often call 'real vanilla'.
